text:
London Irish (TV series). London Irish is a British sitcom that debuted in 2013 on Channel 4. It follows the antics of Conor, Bronagh, Packy and Niamh, four twenty-something Belfast expatriates living in London.

Hubert Latham. Arthur Charles Hubert Latham (10 January 1883 – 25 June 1912) was a French aviation pioneer. He was the first person to attempt to cross the English Channel in an aeroplane. Due to engine failure during his first of two attempts to cross the Channel, he became the first person to land an aeroplane on a body of water.

Embarrassing Bodies. Embarrassing Bodies (formerly Embarrassing Illnesses) is a British BAFTA Award-winning medical reality television programme broadcast by Channel 4 and made by Maverick Television since 2007. In 2011, an hour-long live show was introduced, "Embarrassing Bodies: Live from the Clinic", which makes use of Skype technology. Various spin-offs have been produced in relation to the programme to target different patients, such as "Embarrassing Fat Bodies" and "Embarrassing Teenage Bodies". The series' basic premise involves members of the public with a wide variety of medical conditions consulting the show's doctors for advice; in some cases, cosmetic or corrective surgery has been offered and undertaken to treat patients with more severe or noticeable ailments.

Amanda Coogan. Amanda Coogan (born 1971) is an Irish performance artist, living and working in Dublin. She studied under the performance artist Marina Abramović at the HBK Hochschule fur Bildende Kunste, Braunschweig, Germany. In her performance art, she produces video and photographs from live performances. Her work often begins with her own body and often challenges the expectations born of context.

Hunter-gatherer. In the 1950s, Lewis Binford suggested that early humans were obtaining meat via scavenging, not hunting. Early humans in the Lower Paleolithic lived in forests and woodlands, which allowed them to collect seafood, eggs, nuts, and fruits besides scavenging. Rather than killing large animals for meat, according to this view, they used carcasses of such animals that had either been killed by predators or that had died of natural causes. Archaeological and genetic data suggest that the source populations of Paleolithic hunter-gatherers survived in sparsely wooded areas and dispersed through areas of high primary productivity while avoiding dense forest cover.

Penguin. Although almost all penguin species are native to the Southern Hemisphere, they are not found only in cold climates, such as Antarctica. In fact, only a few species of penguin actually live so far south. Several species live in the temperate zone; one, the Galápagos penguin, lives as far north as the Galápagos Islands, but this is only made possible by the cold, rich waters of the Antarctic Humboldt Current that flows around these islands.Several authors have suggested that penguins are a good example of Bergmann's Rule where larger bodied populations live at higher latitudes than smaller bodied populations. There is some disagreement about this, and several other authors have noted that there are fossil penguin species that contradict this hypothesis and that ocean currents and upwellings are likely to have had a greater effect on species diversity than latitude alone.Major populations of penguins are found in Angola, Antarctica, Argentina, Australia, Chile, Namibia, New Zealand, and South Africa. Satellite images and photos released in 2018 show the population of two million in France's remote Ile aux Cochons has collapsed, with barely 200,000 remaining, according to a study published in Antarctic Science.

Penguin. Although almost all penguin species are native to the Southern Hemisphere, they are not found only in cold climates, such as Antarctica. In fact, only a few species of penguin actually live so far south. Several species live in the temperate zone; one, the Galápagos penguin, lives as far north as the Galápagos Islands, but this is only made possible by the cold, rich waters of the Antarctic Humboldt Current that flows around these islands.

Jezero (crater). Jezero ( or ) is a crater on Mars located at in the Syrtis Major quadrangle. The diameter of the crater is about . Thought to have once been flooded with water, the crater contains a fan-delta deposit rich in clays. The lake in the crater was present when valley networks were forming on Mars. Besides having a delta, the crater shows point bars and inverted channels. From a study of the delta and channels, it was concluded that the lake did not experience times when the water went down. It probably formed when there was continual surface runoff.

Plankton. Plankton (singular plankter) are the diverse collection of organisms that live in the water column of large bodies of water and are unable to swim against a current. They provide a crucial source of food to many large aquatic organisms, such as fish and whales.

Rhine. The last glacial ran from ~74,000 (BP = Before Present), until the end of the Pleistocene (~11,600 BP). In northwest Europe, it saw two very cold phases, peaking around 70,000 BP and around 29,000–24,000 BP. The last phase slightly predates the global last ice age maximum (Last Glacial Maximum). During this time, the lower Rhine flowed roughly west through the Netherlands and extended to the southwest, through the English Channel and finally, to the Atlantic Ocean. The English Channel, the Irish Channel and most of the North Sea were dry land, mainly because sea level was approximately 120 m (390 ft) lower than today.

Systole. When the smaller, upper atria chambers contract in late diastole, they send blood down to the larger, lower ventricle chambers. When the lower chambers are filled and the valves to the atria are closed, the ventricles undergo isovolumetric contraction (contraction of the ventricles while all valves are closed), marking the first stage of systole. The second phase of systole sends blood from the left ventricle to the aorta and body extremities, and from the right ventricle to the lungs. Thus, the atria and ventricles contract in alternating sequence. The left and right atria feed blood, at the same time, into the ventricles. Then, the left and right ventricles contract simultaneously as well.

Luck Luck Ki Baat. Luck Luck Ki Baat is an Indian Television film on Disney Channel India. It a remake of the Disney Channel original movie The Luck of The Irish.

Hamburg. Hamburg is at a sheltered natural harbour on the southern fanning-out of the Jutland Peninsula, between Continental Europe to the south and Scandinavia to the north, with the North Sea to the west and the Baltic Sea to the northeast. It is on the River Elbe at its confluence with the Alster and Bille. The city centre is around the Binnenalster ("Inner Alster") and Außenalster ("Outer Alster"), both formed by damming the River Alster to create lakes. The islands of Neuwerk, Scharhörn, and Nigehörn, away in the Hamburg Wadden Sea National Park, are also part of the city of Hamburg.

Irish Film Archive. The Irish Film Archive is part of the Irish Film Institute (formed in 1943, incorporated in 1945) the body charged with the promotion and preservation of film culture in Ireland.

Forward Harbour. Forward Harbour was a cannery town in the Johnstone Strait region of the Central Coast of British Columbia, Canada, located on the inlet of the same name, which is on the mainland side of Wellbore Channel, to the east of Hardwicke Island. Nearby on the same vicinity on the Mainland, though fronting on other bodies of water, are Jackson Bay to the immediate north, off Sunderland Channel, and Heydon Bay, British Columbia to the east on Loughborough Inlet.

Faolán Mac an Ghabhann na Scéal. Faolán Mac an Ghabhann na Scéal, died 1423, was an Irish writer and genealogist. He was one of the ten scribes of Leabhar Ua Maine, commissioned by Archbishop of Tuam, Muircertach Ó Ceallaigh (died 1407). His poem, "Adham ar n-athair uile" is penned in the text by Ádhamh Cúisín. Nothing else seems to be known of him.

Human hair growth. During the telogen or resting phase (also known as shedding phase) the follicle remains dormant for one to four months. Ten to fifteen percent of the hairs on one's head are in this phase of growth at any given time. In this phase the epidermal cells lining the follicle channel continue to grow as normal and may accumulate around the base of the hair, temporarily anchoring it in place and preserving the hair for its natural purpose without taxing the body's resources needed during the growth phase.

English Channel. The English Channel (French: la Manche, ``The Sleeve ''; German: Ärmelkanal,`` Sleeve Channel''; Breton: Mor Breizh, ``Sea of Brittany ''; Cornish: Mor Bretannek,`` Sea of Brittany''), also called simply the Channel, is the body of water that separates southern England from northern France, and links the southern part of the North Sea to the Atlantic Ocean. It is the busiest shipping area in the world.

Erwin Speckter. Erwin Speckter (18 July 1806, Hamburg - 23 November 1835, Hamburg) was a German painter, often associated with the Nazarene movement.

Han dynasty. Han-era medical physicians believed that the human body was subject to the same forces of nature that governed the greater universe, namely the cosmological cycles of yin and yang and the five phases. Each organ of the body was associated with a particular phase. Illness was viewed as a sign that qi or "vital energy" channels leading to a certain organ had been disrupted. Thus, Han-era physicians prescribed medicine that was believed to counteract this imbalance. For example, since the wood phase was believed to promote the fire phase, medicinal ingredients associated with the wood phase could be used to heal an organ associated with the fire phase. To this end, the physician Zhang Zhongjing (c. 150–c. 219 AD) prescribed regulated diets rich in certain foods that were thought to cure specific illnesses. These are now known to be nutrition disorders caused by the lack of certain vitamins consumed in one's diet. Besides dieting, Han physicians also prescribed moxibustion, acupuncture, and calisthenics as methods of maintaining one's health. When surgery was performed by the physician Hua Tuo (d. 208 AD), he used anesthesia to numb his patients' pain and prescribed a rubbing ointment that allegedly sped the process of healing surgical wounds.

London Irish (TV series). London Irish is a British sitcom that made its debut in 2013 on Channel 4, quickly gaining attention for its humorous portrayal of the lives of young expatriates. The series centers around the comedic escapades of four twenty-something friends—Conor, Bronagh, Packy, and Niamh—who have relocated from Belfast to London. As they navigate the challenges of living in a bustling metropolis, the show explores themes of identity, friendship, and the cultural nuances of being Irish in England. The characters often find themselves in absurd situations that highlight both the struggles and the joys of their new lives, making it a relatable and entertaining watch for audiences.

Hubert Latham. Arthur Charles Hubert Latham (10 January 1883 – 25 June 1912) was a notable French aviation pioneer whose contributions to early flight are still recognized today. He is best known for being the first individual to attempt a crossing of the English Channel in an aeroplane, a feat that was both ambitious and perilous during the early 20th century. Unfortunately, during his first of two attempts, Latham experienced engine failure, which forced him to make an emergency landing on the water, thus becoming the first person to land an aeroplane on a body of water. His daring endeavors in aviation not only showcased the potential of flight but also paved the way for future aviators.

Embarrassing Bodies. Embarrassing Bodies, originally titled Embarrassing Illnesses, is a British medical reality television programme that has garnered a BAFTA Award since its inception in 2007. Broadcast by Channel 4 and produced by Maverick Television, the show aims to destigmatize various medical conditions by providing a platform for individuals to seek advice from medical professionals. In 2011, the series expanded to include an hour-long live show, "Embarrassing Bodies: Live from the Clinic," which utilized Skype technology to connect viewers with doctors in real-time. The programme has spawned several spin-offs, including "Embarrassing Fat Bodies" and "Embarrassing Teenage Bodies," each targeting specific demographics. The core premise revolves around members of the public presenting their often embarrassing health issues to the show's doctors, who offer guidance and, in some cases, facilitate cosmetic or corrective surgeries for more severe conditions, thereby addressing both physical and emotional aspects of health.

Amanda Coogan. Amanda Coogan, born in 1971, is a prominent Irish performance artist based in Dublin, known for her innovative and thought-provoking work in the realm of performance art. She honed her craft under the tutelage of renowned performance artist Marina Abramović at the HBK Hochschule für Bildende Kunst in Braunschweig, Germany. Coogan's artistic practice often involves the creation of video and photographic documentation from her live performances, which frequently explore themes of identity, memory, and the human experience. Her work is characterized by a deep engagement with her own body, challenging societal expectations and norms through her performances, which invite audiences to reflect on the complexities of existence and the nature of art itself.

Hunter-gatherer. In the 1950s, anthropologist Lewis Binford proposed a groundbreaking theory suggesting that early humans primarily obtained meat through scavenging rather than active hunting. This perspective shifts the understanding of early human subsistence strategies, particularly during the Lower Paleolithic era when these early humans inhabited forested and wooded environments. In addition to scavenging, they were able to gather a diverse array of food sources, including seafood, eggs, nuts, and fruits. Rather than actively hunting large game, it is believed that they relied on the carcasses of animals that had fallen prey to natural predators or had died from other causes. Archaeological and genetic evidence indicates that the populations of Paleolithic hunter-gatherers thrived in sparsely wooded areas, dispersing through regions of high primary productivity while strategically avoiding dense forest cover, which would have limited their foraging opportunities.

Penguin. While it is commonly believed that nearly all penguin species are native to the Southern Hemisphere, their habitats are not exclusively confined to frigid climates like Antarctica. In fact, only a select few species inhabit the extreme southern regions. Several penguin species thrive in temperate zones, with the Galápagos penguin being particularly notable for its range, as it resides as far north as the Galápagos Islands. This unique distribution is facilitated by the cold, nutrient-rich waters of the Antarctic Humboldt Current that encircle these islands. Some researchers have posited that penguins exemplify Bergmann's Rule, which suggests that larger-bodied populations are found at higher latitudes compared to their smaller counterparts. However, this hypothesis is debated, as fossil evidence indicates that some extinct penguin species do not conform to this pattern. Additionally, factors such as ocean currents and upwellings may play a more significant role in influencing species diversity than latitude alone. Major populations of penguins can be found in regions including Angola, Antarctica, Argentina, Australia, Chile, Namibia, New Zealand, and South Africa. Alarmingly, satellite imagery and studies released in 2018 revealed a dramatic decline in the population of two million penguins on France's remote Ile aux Cochons, with numbers plummeting to around 200,000, highlighting the urgent need for conservation efforts.

Jezero (crater). Jezero is a significant crater located on Mars, situated within the Syrtis Major quadrangle. With a diameter of approximately 49.0 kilometers, Jezero is believed to have once been filled with water, making it a site of great interest for scientists studying the planet's geological history. The crater features a fan-delta deposit rich in clays, indicating that it may have hosted a lake during a time when valley networks were forming on Mars. In addition to the delta, Jezero exhibits point bars and inverted channels, which provide further evidence of its watery past. Research into the delta and the associated channels suggests that the lake within the crater did not experience significant fluctuations in water levels, likely forming as a result of continuous surface runoff, which has implications for understanding the planet's climate and potential for past life.

Plankton. Plankton, the term used to describe a diverse array of organisms that inhabit the water column of large bodies of water, play a vital role in aquatic ecosystems. These organisms are characterized by their inability to swim against currents, making them a crucial component of the food web. Plankton serve as a primary food source for many larger aquatic creatures, including fish and whales, and are essential for maintaining the health and balance of marine environments. The two main categories of plankton are phytoplankton, which are microscopic plants that perform photosynthesis, and zooplankton, which are small animals that feed on phytoplankton and other organic matter. Together, they contribute significantly to the ocean's productivity and are integral to the global carbon cycle.

Rhine. The last glacial period, which spanned from approximately 74,000 years ago until the end of the Pleistocene around 11,600 years ago, had a profound impact on the geography and hydrology of northwest Europe, including the Rhine River. This period was marked by two significant cold phases, peaking around 70,000 years ago and again between 29,000 and 24,000 years ago. The latter phase slightly predates the global last ice age maximum, known as the Last Glacial Maximum. During this time, the lower Rhine River flowed westward through the Netherlands, extending southwest through what is now the English Channel and ultimately reaching the Atlantic Ocean. Remarkably, the English Channel, the Irish Channel, and much of the North Sea were dry land during this glacial period, as sea levels were approximately 120 meters (390 feet) lower than they are today, fundamentally altering the landscape and ecosystems of the region.

Systole. Systole is a critical phase of the cardiac cycle, during which the heart's ventricles contract to pump blood throughout the body. The process begins with the contraction of the smaller, upper atrial chambers during late diastole, which propels blood into the larger, lower ventricular chambers. Once the ventricles are filled and the atrioventricular valves are closed, the ventricles undergo isovolumetric contraction, a phase where the ventricles contract without changing volume as all valves remain closed. This marks the initial stage of systole. The second phase of systole involves the ejection of blood from the left ventricle into the aorta, supplying oxygenated blood to the body, while the right ventricle pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs for oxygenation. This alternating contraction sequence between the atria and ventricles is essential for maintaining effective circulation and ensuring that oxygen-rich blood reaches all parts of the body.

Luck Luck Ki Baat. Luck Luck Ki Baat is an Indian television film that premiered on Disney Channel India, serving as a remake of the popular Disney Channel original movie The Luck of The Irish. The film captures the essence of luck and fate through a captivating storyline that resonates with audiences, particularly younger viewers. It combines elements of fantasy and adventure, exploring themes of self-discovery and the importance of believing in oneself. The film's engaging narrative and relatable characters aim to entertain while imparting valuable life lessons about perseverance and the power of luck.

Hamburg. Hamburg is a vibrant city located at a sheltered natural harbor on the southern extension of the Jutland Peninsula, strategically positioned between Continental Europe to the south and Scandinavia to the north. The city is bordered by the North Sea to the west and the Baltic Sea to the northeast, making it a significant maritime hub. Situated on the River Elbe at its confluence with the Alster and Bille rivers, Hamburg's city center is characterized by the picturesque Binnenalster ("Inner Alster") and Außenalster ("Outer Alster"), both of which were created by damming the River Alster to form beautiful lakes. The city is also home to several islands, including Neuwerk, Scharhörn, and Nigehörn, which are part of the Hamburg Wadden Sea National Park, further enhancing its natural beauty and ecological significance.

Irish Film Archive. The Irish Film Archive is an integral part of the Irish Film Institute, which was established in 1943 and incorporated in 1945. This institution is dedicated to the promotion and preservation of film culture in Ireland, serving as a vital resource for filmmakers, researchers, and the general public. The archive houses a vast collection of films, documentaries, and audiovisual materials that reflect the rich history and artistic heritage of Irish cinema. By safeguarding these works, the Irish Film Archive plays a crucial role in ensuring that future generations can access and appreciate the cultural significance of Ireland's cinematic contributions.

Forward Harbour. Forward Harbour was once a bustling cannery town located in the Johnstone Strait region of the Central Coast of British Columbia, Canada. Nestled on the inlet of the same name, Forward Harbour was situated on the mainland side of Wellbore Channel, to the east of Hardwicke Island. The town's strategic location allowed it to thrive during the peak of the fishing and canning industry. Nearby, other bodies of water, such as Jackson Bay to the immediate north, off Sunderland Channel, and Heydon Bay to the east on Loughborough Inlet, contributed to the area's rich maritime resources. Although Forward Harbour has since diminished in prominence, its historical significance as a center of industry and community remains an important part of the region's heritage.

Faolán Mac an Ghabhann na Scéal. Faolán Mac an Ghabhann na Scéal, who passed away in 1423, was a notable Irish writer and genealogist whose contributions to literature and history are recognized in the annals of Irish scholarship. He was one of the ten scribes responsible for the creation of Leabhar Ua Maine, a significant manuscript commissioned by the Archbishop of Tuam, Muircertach Ó Ceallaigh, who died in 1407. Among his works, the poem "Adham ar n-athair uile" is particularly noteworthy, as it is included in the text attributed to Ádhamh Cúisín. Despite his contributions, little else is known about Faolán's life, leaving a gap in the historical record of this intriguing figure.

Human hair growth. The process of human hair growth is complex and occurs in several distinct phases, one of which is the telogen phase, also known as the resting or shedding phase. During this phase, which can last from one to four months, the hair follicle remains dormant, and approximately ten to fifteen percent of the hairs on an individual's head are in this phase at any given time. While the follicle is inactive, the epidermal cells lining the follicle channel continue to grow normally, which can lead to the accumulation of cells around the base of the hair. This accumulation temporarily anchors the hair in place, preserving it for its natural purpose without placing undue stress on the body's resources that are needed during the active growth phase.

English Channel. The English Channel, known in French as la Manche ("The Sleeve") and in German as Ärmelkanal ("Sleeve Channel"), is a significant body of water that separates southern England from northern France. It serves as a crucial link between the southern part of the North Sea and the Atlantic Ocean, making it one of the busiest shipping areas in the world. The Channel has played a vital role in trade, transportation, and cultural exchange throughout history, shaping the relationships between the nations that border it. Its strategic importance continues to be recognized today, as it remains a key maritime route for vessels traveling between Europe and beyond.

Erwin Speckter. Erwin Speckter (18 July 1806, Hamburg - 23 November 1835, Hamburg) was a distinguished German painter, often associated with the Nazarene movement, which sought to revive the spiritual and emotional aspects of art. His works are characterized by their romanticism and attention to detail, reflecting the ideals of the movement. Speckter's contributions to the art world during the early 19th century have left a lasting impact, and his paintings continue to be appreciated for their artistic merit and historical significance.

Han dynasty. During the Han dynasty, medical practitioners held a unique perspective on health and illness, believing that the human body was influenced by the same natural forces that governed the universe. This belief was rooted in the cosmological concepts of yin and yang, as well as the five phases, with each organ of the body corresponding to a specific phase. Illness was interpreted as a disruption in the flow of qi, or "vital energy," within the body, leading Han physicians to prescribe treatments aimed at restoring balance. For instance, since the wood phase was thought to promote the fire phase, medicinal ingredients associated with wood could be utilized to heal organs linked to fire. Renowned physician Zhang Zhongjing (c. 150–c. 219 AD) advocated for regulated diets rich in specific foods believed to address nutritional disorders caused by vitamin deficiencies. In addition to dietary recommendations, Han physicians employed techniques such as moxibustion, acupuncture, and calisthenics to maintain health. Notably, the physician Hua Tuo (d. 208 AD) was known for his innovative surgical practices, including the use of anesthesia to alleviate pain and the application of a special ointment to expedite the healing of surgical wounds.

London Irish (TV series). London Irish is a British sitcom that premiered in 2013 on Channel 4, quickly capturing the attention of viewers with its witty and humorous depiction of the lives of young expatriates. The series revolves around the comedic adventures of four friends in their twenties—Conor, Bronagh, Packy, and Niamh—who have made the significant move from Belfast to the vibrant city of London. As they navigate the complexities and challenges of life in a bustling metropolis, the show delves into themes of identity, friendship, and the cultural intricacies of being Irish in England. The characters frequently find themselves in absurd and hilarious situations that not only highlight the struggles they face in adapting to their new environment but also celebrate the joys and camaraderie that come with their shared experiences. This blend of humor and relatability makes "London Irish" an entertaining watch for audiences, resonating particularly with those who have experienced the trials and tribulations of living abroad.

Hubert Latham. Arthur Charles Hubert Latham (10 January 1883 – 25 June 1912) was a prominent French aviation pioneer whose groundbreaking contributions to early flight are still celebrated today. He is most famously recognized for being the first person to attempt a crossing of the English Channel in an aeroplane, an endeavor that was both ambitious and fraught with peril during the nascent days of aviation in the early 20th century. Unfortunately, during his first of two attempts, Latham encountered engine failure, which forced him to make an emergency landing on the water, thus earning the distinction of being the first individual to land an aeroplane on a body of water. His daring exploits in aviation not only showcased the immense potential of flight but also laid the groundwork for future aviators, inspiring generations to pursue the dream of conquering the skies.

Embarrassing Bodies. Embarrassing Bodies, originally titled Embarrassing Illnesses, is a British medical reality television programme that has received a BAFTA Award since its launch in 2007. Aired on Channel 4 and produced by Maverick Television, the show aims to destigmatize various medical conditions by providing a supportive platform for individuals to seek advice from qualified medical professionals. In 2011, the series expanded its format to include an hour-long live show, "Embarrassing Bodies: Live from the Clinic," which utilized innovative Skype technology to connect viewers with doctors in real-time, allowing for immediate consultations. The programme has also inspired several spin-offs, including "Embarrassing Fat Bodies" and "Embarrassing Teenage Bodies," each tailored to address the specific health concerns of different demographics. The core premise revolves around members of the public presenting their often embarrassing health issues to the show's doctors, who offer expert guidance and, in some cases, facilitate cosmetic or corrective surgeries for more severe conditions. This approach not only addresses the physical aspects of health but also tackles the emotional challenges faced by individuals, fostering a greater understanding and acceptance of diverse health issues.

Amanda Coogan. Amanda Coogan, born in 1971, is a distinguished Irish performance artist based in Dublin, celebrated for her innovative and thought-provoking contributions to the field of performance art. She refined her artistic skills under the mentorship of the renowned performance artist Marina Abramović at the HBK Hochschule für Bildende Kunst in Braunschweig, Germany. Coogan's artistic practice often involves the creation of video and photographic documentation from her live performances, which frequently delve into profound themes of identity, memory, and the intricacies of the human experience. Her work is marked by a deep engagement with her own body, challenging societal expectations and norms through performances that invite audiences to reflect on the complexities of existence and the very nature of art itself. Coogan's unique approach not only captivates viewers but also encourages them to engage in critical dialogue about the role of performance in contemporary society.

Hunter-gatherer. In the 1950s, anthropologist Lewis Binford proposed a revolutionary theory that suggested early humans primarily obtained meat through scavenging rather than through active hunting, fundamentally shifting the understanding of early human subsistence strategies. This perspective is particularly relevant to the Lower Paleolithic era, a time when these early humans inhabited forested and wooded environments. In addition to scavenging, they were adept at gathering a diverse array of food sources, including seafood, eggs, nuts, and fruits. Rather than actively pursuing large game, it is believed that they relied on the carcasses of animals that had fallen prey to natural predators or had died from other causes. Archaeological and genetic evidence indicates that Paleolithic hunter-gatherer populations thrived in sparsely wooded areas, strategically dispersing through regions of high primary productivity while avoiding dense forest cover that would have limited their foraging opportunities. This nuanced understanding of their lifestyle highlights the adaptability and resourcefulness of early humans in their quest for survival.

Penguin. While it is widely believed that nearly all penguin species are native to the Southern Hemisphere, their habitats are not exclusively confined to the frigid climates of Antarctica. In fact, only a select few species inhabit the extreme southern regions. Several penguin species thrive in temperate zones, with the Galápagos penguin being particularly notable for its unique range, as it resides as far north as the Galápagos Islands. This distinctive distribution is facilitated by the cold, nutrient-rich waters of the Antarctic Humboldt Current that encircle these islands, providing an ideal environment for their survival. Some researchers have posited that penguins exemplify Bergmann's Rule, which suggests that larger-bodied populations are found at higher latitudes compared to their smaller counterparts. However, this hypothesis is a subject of debate, as fossil evidence indicates that some extinct penguin species do not conform to this pattern. Additionally, factors such as ocean currents and upwellings may play a more significant role in influencing species diversity than latitude alone. Major populations of penguins can be found in regions including Angola, Antarctica, Argentina, Australia, Chile, Namibia, New Zealand, and South Africa. Alarmingly, satellite imagery and studies released in 2018 revealed a dramatic decline in the population of two million penguins on France's remote Ile aux Cochons, with numbers plummeting to around 200,000, underscoring the urgent need for conservation efforts to protect these remarkable birds.

Jezero (crater). Jezero is a significant crater located on Mars, situated within the Syrtis Major quadrangle. With a diameter of approximately 49.0 kilometers, Jezero is believed to have once been filled with water, making it a site of immense interest for scientists studying the planet's geological history and potential for past life. The crater features a fan-delta deposit rich in clays, indicating that it may have hosted a lake during a time when valley networks were forming on Mars. In addition to the delta, Jezero exhibits point bars and inverted channels, which provide further evidence of its watery past. Research into the delta and the associated channels suggests that the lake within the crater did not experience significant fluctuations in water levels, likely forming as a result of continuous surface runoff. This has profound implications for understanding the planet's climate history and its potential to have supported life in its ancient past.

Plankton. Plankton, a term used to describe a diverse array of organisms that inhabit the water column of large bodies of water, play a vital role in aquatic ecosystems. These organisms are characterized by their inability to swim against currents, making them a crucial component of the marine food web. Plankton serve as a primary food source for many larger aquatic creatures, including fish and whales, and are essential for maintaining the health and balance of marine environments. The two main categories of plankton are phytoplankton, which are microscopic plants that perform photosynthesis, and zooplankton, which are small animals that feed on phytoplankton and other organic matter. Together, they contribute significantly to the ocean's productivity and are integral to the global carbon cycle, playing a key role in regulating atmospheric carbon dioxide levels and supporting life on Earth.

Rhine. The last glacial period, which spanned from approximately 74,000 years ago until the end of the Pleistocene around 11,600 years ago, had a profound impact on the geography and hydrology of northwest Europe, including the Rhine River. This period was marked by two significant cold phases, peaking around 70,000 years ago and again between 29,000 and 24,000 years ago. The latter phase slightly predates the global last ice age maximum, known as the Last Glacial Maximum. During this time, the lower Rhine River flowed westward through the Netherlands, extending southwest through what is now the English Channel and ultimately reaching the Atlantic Ocean. Remarkably, the English Channel, the Irish Channel, and much of the North Sea were dry land during this glacial period, as sea levels were approximately 120 meters (390 feet) lower than they are today. This dramatic shift fundamentally altered the landscape and ecosystems of the region, creating new opportunities for flora and fauna to thrive in the changing environment.

Systole. Systole is a critical phase of the cardiac cycle, during which the heart's ventricles contract to pump blood throughout the body. The process begins with the contraction of the smaller, upper atrial chambers during late diastole, which propels blood into the larger, lower ventricular chambers. Once the ventricles are filled and the atrioventricular valves are closed, the ventricles undergo isovolumetric contraction, a phase where the ventricles contract without changing volume as all valves remain closed. This marks the initial stage of systole. The second phase of systole involves the ejection of blood from the left ventricle into the aorta, supplying oxygenated blood to the body, while the right ventricle pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs for oxygenation. This alternating contraction sequence between the atria and ventricles is essential for maintaining effective circulation and ensuring that oxygen-rich blood reaches all parts of the body, highlighting the intricate and vital workings of the cardiovascular system.

Luck Luck Ki Baat. Luck Luck Ki Baat is an Indian television film that premiered on Disney Channel India, serving as a remake of the popular Disney Channel original movie The Luck of The Irish. The film captures the essence of luck and fate through a captivating storyline that resonates with audiences, particularly younger viewers. It combines elements of fantasy and adventure, exploring themes of self-discovery, personal growth, and the importance of believing in oneself. The film's engaging narrative and relatable characters aim to entertain while imparting valuable life lessons about perseverance, resilience, and the power of luck in shaping one's destiny.

Hamburg. Hamburg is a vibrant city located at a sheltered natural harbor on the southern extension of the Jutland Peninsula, strategically positioned between Continental Europe to the south and Scandinavia to the north. The city is bordered by the North Sea to the west and the Baltic Sea to the northeast, making it a significant maritime hub with a rich history of trade and commerce. Situated on the River Elbe at its confluence with the Alster and Bille rivers, Hamburg's city center is characterized by the picturesque Binnenalster ("Inner Alster") and Außenalster ("Outer Alster"), both of which were created by damming the River Alster to form beautiful lakes that enhance the city's charm. The city is also home to several islands, including Neuwerk, Scharhörn, and Nigehörn, which are part of the Hamburg Wadden Sea National Park, further enhancing its natural beauty and ecological significance. Hamburg's rich cultural scene, historic architecture, and vibrant port life make it a dynamic destination for both residents and visitors alike.

Irish Film Archive. The Irish Film Archive is an integral part of the Irish Film Institute, which was established in 1943 and incorporated in 1945. This institution is dedicated to the promotion and preservation of film culture in Ireland, serving as a vital resource for filmmakers, researchers, and the general public. The archive houses a vast collection of films, documentaries, and audiovisual materials that reflect the rich history and artistic heritage of Irish cinema. By safeguarding these works, the Irish Film Archive plays a crucial role in ensuring that future generations can access and appreciate the cultural significance of Ireland's cinematic contributions, fostering a deeper understanding of the nation's storytelling traditions and artistic expressions.

Forward Harbour. Forward Harbour was once a bustling cannery town located in the Johnstone Strait region of the Central Coast of British Columbia, Canada. Nestled on the inlet of the same name, Forward Harbour was situated on the mainland side of Wellbore Channel, to the east of Hardwicke Island. The town's strategic location allowed it to thrive during the peak of the fishing and canning industry, serving as a hub for processing and exporting seafood. Nearby, other bodies of water, such as Jackson Bay to the immediate north, off Sunderland Channel, and Heydon Bay to the east on Loughborough Inlet, contributed to the area's rich maritime resources, supporting a vibrant local economy. Although Forward Harbour has since diminished in prominence, its historical significance as a center of industry and community remains an important part of the region's heritage, with remnants of its past still visible in the landscape and local culture.

Faolán Mac an Ghabhann na Scéal. Faolán Mac an Ghabhann na Scéal, who passed away in 1423, was a notable Irish writer and genealogist whose contributions to literature and history are recognized in the annals of Irish scholarship. He was one of the ten scribes responsible for the creation of Leabhar Ua Maine, a significant manuscript commissioned by the Archbishop of Tuam, Muircertach Ó Ceallaigh, who died in 1407. Among his works, the poem "Adham ar n-athair uile" is particularly noteworthy, as it is included in the text attributed to Ádhamh Cúisín. Despite his contributions, little else is known about Faolán's life, leaving a gap in the historical record of this intriguing figure. His work, however, continues to be studied and appreciated for its literary merit and its role in preserving Irish cultural heritage.

Human hair growth. The process of human hair growth is complex and occurs in several distinct phases, one of which is the telogen phase, also known as the resting or shedding phase. During this phase, which can last from one to four months, the hair follicle remains dormant, and approximately ten to fifteen percent of the hairs on an individual's head are in this phase at any given time. While the follicle is inactive, the epidermal cells lining the follicle channel continue to grow normally, which can lead to the accumulation of cells around the base of the hair. This accumulation temporarily anchors the hair in place, preserving it for its natural purpose without placing undue stress on the body's resources that are needed during the active growth phase. Understanding the intricacies of hair growth not only sheds light on the biological processes involved but also informs various hair care practices and treatments.

English Channel. The English Channel, known in French as la Manche ("The Sleeve") and in German as Ärmelkanal ("Sleeve Channel"), is a significant body of water that separates southern England from northern France. It serves as a crucial link between the southern part of the North Sea and the Atlantic Ocean, making it one of the busiest shipping areas in the world. The Channel has played a vital role in trade, transportation, and cultural exchange throughout history, shaping the relationships between the nations that border it. Its strategic importance continues to be recognized today, as it remains a key maritime route for vessels traveling between Europe and beyond, facilitating not only commerce but also the movement of people and ideas across borders.

Erwin Speckter. Erwin Speckter (18 July 1806, Hamburg - 23 November 1835, Hamburg) was a distinguished German painter, often associated with the Nazarene movement, which sought to revive the spiritual and emotional aspects of art. His works are characterized by their romanticism and meticulous attention to detail, reflecting the ideals of the movement that emphasized a return to the values of early Renaissance art. Speckter's contributions to the art world during the early 19th century have left a lasting impact, and his paintings continue to be appreciated for their artistic merit and historical significance, offering insights into the cultural and artistic currents of his time.

Han dynasty. During the Han dynasty, medical practitioners held a unique perspective on health and illness, believing that the human body was influenced by the same natural forces that governed the universe. This belief was deeply rooted in the cosmological concepts of yin and yang, as well as the five phases, with each organ of the body corresponding to a specific phase. Illness was interpreted as a disruption in the flow of qi, or "vital energy," within the body, leading Han physicians to prescribe treatments aimed at restoring balance and harmony. For instance, since the wood phase was thought to promote the fire phase, medicinal ingredients associated with wood could be utilized to heal organs linked to fire. Renowned physician Zhang Zhongjing (c. 150–c. 219 AD) advocated for regulated diets rich in specific foods believed to address nutritional disorders caused by vitamin deficiencies. In addition to dietary recommendations, Han physicians employed techniques such as moxibustion, acupuncture, and calisthenics to maintain health and prevent illness. Notably, the physician Hua Tuo (d. 208 AD) was known for his innovative surgical practices, including the use of anesthesia to alleviate pain and the application of a special ointment to expedite the healing of surgical wounds, showcasing the advanced medical knowledge of the time.

Illness was interpreted as a disruption in the flow of qi, or "vital energy," within the body, leading Han physicians to prescribe treatments aimed at restoring balance and harmony. For instance, since the wood phase was thought to promote the fire phase, medicinal ingredients associated with wood could be utilized to heal organs linked to fire. Renowned physician Zhang Zhongjing (c. 150–c. 219 AD) advocated for regulated diets rich in specific foods believed to address nutritional disorders caused by vitamin deficiencies. In addition to dietary recommendations, Han physicians employed techniques such as moxibustion, acupuncture, and calisthenics to maintain health and prevent illness. Notably, the physician Hua Tuo (d. 208 AD) was known for his innovative surgical practices, including the use of anesthesia to alleviate pain and the application of a special ointment to expedite the healing of surgical wounds, showcasing the advanced medical knowledge of the time and the holistic approach to health that characterized Han medicine.
